Swiss CitiesVals

Vals

GRGraubünden

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The stone that remembers

A child touches the quartzite wall of the Therme Vals and hears the mountain's memories: the ancient sea where the stone formed millions of years ago, the Walser builders who first used it for their houses, the quarry workers who cut it free, and finally Peter Zumthor laying the first slab. Each of the 60,000 stones has a story, and the mountain wants them all told.

Create This Story

Peter Zumthor's Therme Vals, opened on 14 December 1996, is clad in 60,000 stone slabs of Valser quartzite quarried from the valley itself. The slabs come in three consistent thicknesses -- 31 mm, 47 mm, and 63 mm -- and the total of the three always equals 15 cm. Valser quartzite is a light grey and greenish fine-grained gneiss formed from granite metamorphosed during the Alpine orogeny. The primary quarrying company, Truffer AG, founded in 1983, extracts approximately 7,000 cubic meters per year from the Liemschboda quarry. In 2009, Zumthor was awarded the Pritzker Architecture Prize.

The mountain porters of Vals

A child joins the legendary Valser Saumtreiber on a winter journey over the Valserberg pass, carrying salt and cheese through snowstorms to the distant markets of Bellinzona. Along the treacherous route, the porters share stories of the isolated valley, where every household depended on these brave men to cross the mountains before the road to Ilanz changed everything in 1880.

Create This Story

For centuries, the economy of Vals was based on subsistence agriculture and cattle breeding in harsh high-Alpine conditions. The famous Valser mountain porters (Saumtreiber) carried heavy loads over the Valserberg pass to the south, especially in winter when pack animals could not manage the snow-covered passes. Livestock was driven to markets in Bellinzona and Lugano. Until the construction of the road to Ilanz in 1879--1880, all goods transport ran southward over the Valserberg -- the valley had no direct northern road connection.

The warm spring's secret

A child follows a trickle of warm water from the Therme's Drinking Stone back through the mountain, discovering an underground world where the water has journeyed for a hundred years through layers of ancient rock. From raindrop on a distant peak to warm spring at 30 degrees, the child traces the water's century-long path and learns why the mountain gives this gift to the valley.

Create This Story

The hot mineral spring in Vals (the St. Petersquelle) was first mentioned by name in 1622, though local knowledge of the warm waters likely predates this. The natural spring water emerges at 30 degrees Celsius at an elevation of 1,252 metres. The water takes 25 to 100 years to travel from mountain rain and snow through the rock to emerge as crystal-clear mineral water. The Therme Vals features chambers at different temperatures: the indoor pool (32 degrees C), outdoor pool (36 degrees C in winter), Fire Bath (42 degrees C), Ice Bath (14 degrees C), Flower Bath (33 degrees C), Sound Bath (35 degrees C), and the Drinking Stone with natural ferrous spring water at 29.8 degrees C.

The village under the lake

At the Zervreilasee, the water level drops in autumn and a child glimpses the drowned village of Zervreila below the surface. Diving into the turquoise water, the child hears the stories of the families who once lived there -- and learns how the valley chose to sacrifice one village to build a future, just years after the devastating 1951 avalanche had nearly destroyed Vals itself.

Create This Story

The Zervreilasee is a reservoir located southwest of Vals at an elevation of 1,862 metres. The Zervreila Dam is a concrete arch dam built between 1953 and 1957, standing 151 metres high with a crest length of 504 metres, impounding the Valserrhein river. The reservoir holds 100.5 million cubic metres of water. The village of Zervreila was submerged when the reservoir filled. Construction of the dam between 1951 and 1958 provided a critical economic stimulus for the valley, bringing jobs and infrastructure at a time when Vals was still reeling from the 1951 avalanche disaster that killed 19 people.

The avalanche chronicle

A child finds the ancient Lawinechronik, kept in the church since 1598, and travels through its pages: witnessing the great flood of 1868 that nearly drove the village to emigrate to America, the terrible Winter of Terror in 1951 when a 300-metre avalanche killed 19 people including 14 children, and the decades of reforestation that finally taught the valley to live with its mountain.

Create This Story

A chronicle of avalanches (die Lawinechronik) has been kept in Vals since 1598. In 1868, catastrophic floods inundated the entire valley floor and destroyed parts of the settlement; the village seriously considered mass emigration to America. On 20 January 1951, during the infamous 'Winter of Terror', a 300-metre-wide avalanche struck Vals, burying 34 people and destroying 23 buildings. 19 people died, including 14 children. The disaster led the municipality to invest CHF 21.5 million in reforestation and protective structures.

The architect and the mountain

Peter Zumthor cannot find the right design for the thermal baths. A child from Vals shows him how the quartzite layers in the mountainside already contain the answer -- the building is already inside the stone, waiting to be uncovered. Together they imagine a structure that grows from the mountain itself, half-buried in the hillside, with light filtering through glass gaps like water through rock.

Create This Story

Peter Zumthor, born 26 April 1943 in Basel, designed the Therme Vals between 1993 and 1996. The building is half-buried in the hillside with a grass-covered roof that merges with the surrounding meadow. It consists of 15 rectangular stone blocks, each 5 metres high, arranged in a grid pattern. The roofs of the blocks do not touch -- 6-to-8-centimetre gaps between them are covered with glass, allowing natural light to filter through as thin luminous lines. Zumthor lived in the hamlet of Leis above Vals for nearly two decades. He described his philosophy: 'Architecture today needs to reflect on the tasks and possibilities which are inherently its own.'

The Walser who walked from Valais

A Walser family leaves the Rhone valley around 1300, crossing high passes with their livestock to discover the remote Vals valley. A child must help them build the first Blockbau house before winter arrives, learning the ancient technique of interlocking logs and raising granaries on mushroom-shaped stone stilts to keep the mice away -- skills that would define this valley for seven hundred years.

Create This Story

The Walser are descendants of Alemannic settlers who migrated from the upper Rhone valley (Valais/Wallis) into the high Alpine valleys between roughly 1150 and 1450. Around 1300, German-speaking Walser from Upper Valais moved into the Vals valley. They received the Walserrecht (colonist right): personal freedom, the right to form their own judicial communities, and hereditary land rights. The Walser brought their distinctive Blockbau (log construction) building tradition -- wooden houses built with interlocking horizontal logs, triangular roofs, and elevated granaries resting on mushroom-shaped stone stilts. The Walser dialect preserves archaisms from Old High German lost in other dialects.

The water that became famous

A raindrop falls on the mountain above Vals and begins a hundred-year journey through layers of ancient rock, slowly becoming mineral water. When it finally emerges at the St. Petersquelle, it must choose: become a bottle of Valser water seen in restaurants around the world, or flow into the ancient Therme baths where it will warm the skin of travellers who came from far away to find it.

Create This Story

Vals has two commercial mineral springs: the St. Petersquelle (at 1,252 m) and the St. Paulsquelle (at 1,815 m, among Switzerland's highest commercial springs). In 1960, entrepreneurs Hess and Schrauder redeveloped the St. Petersquelle, launching the modern Valser brand. The water takes 25 to 100 years to travel through the rock. In 2002, Coca-Cola acquired Valser Mineralquellen AG, and Valser became Switzerland's market-leading mineral water brand. Key milestones include designer glass bottles by Luigi Colani (1990), PET bottles designed by Mario Botta (2001), and 100% recycled rPET bottles in 2019.
